seoTitle: How to Make a Flow Chart in Google Docs (2026)
metaDescription: Learn how to build a flow chart in Google Docs using the built-in Drawing tool's flowchart shapes, connectors, and text labels.
intro: Google Docs has a dedicated flowchart shape set built into its Drawing tool, making it possible to diagram a process or decision path without leaving the document. This guide covers placing shapes, connecting them with arrows, and labeling each step. It's for anyone mapping out a workflow, troubleshooting sequence, or team hierarchy.

steps.heading: Open the Drawing tool
steps.body: Click Insert, hover over Drawing, and click New to launch the canvas.

steps.heading: Select flowchart shapes
steps.body: Click the Shapes selector icon and choose the Flowchart sub-menu to access process and decision shapes.

steps.heading: Place your first shape
steps.body: Select a rounded process terminal shape, click, and drag across the canvas to draw it.

steps.heading: Label and connect your shapes
steps.body: Use the text tool to type a label inside each shape, then use the Line selector with Connector Arrow to link the shapes together in sequence.

steps.heading: Style and save
steps.body: Adjust fills, border weights, and fonts using the top toolbar, then click Save and Close to embed the diagram into your document.

faq.question: Does Google Docs have preset flowchart shapes?
faq.answer: Yes, the Drawing tool includes a dedicated Flowchart shape category with terminals, processes, decisions, and inputs.

faq.question: How do I keep connector arrows attached when moving shapes?
faq.answer: Hold Control or Command to multi-select connected elements together so connections don't break when repositioning.

faq.question: Can I edit a flow chart after saving it?
faq.answer: Yes, double-clicking the embedded drawing reopens the canvas for further edits.

faq.question: Is there a limit to how many shapes I can add?
faq.answer: There's no hard limit, but very large diagrams can become harder to manage within the drawing canvas.
